title: Unexpected PLC Logic CRC Change

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for SR 3.4.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IEC 62443 SR 3.4 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.15.29: Unexpected PLC Logic CRC Change.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.15.29' running on index=ot_eng CRC events; approved_plc_crc.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=ot_eng sourcetype="plc:crc" earliest=-7d
| lookup approved_plc_crc.csv controller program_name OUTPUT expected_crc
| where isnotnull(expected_crc) AND crc!=expected_crc
| stats earliest(_time) as first_bad by controller, program_name, crc, expected_crc, user
| sort - first_bad
description: Compares reported logic checksums or project CRCs to last approved baseline to detect unauthorized software changes.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IEC 62443 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ot_eng and sourcetypes plc:crc. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Export CRC after each validated download; (2) store gold images securely; (3) auto-ticket on mismatch; (4) offline forensic for first event. Indexes required: `index=ot_eng`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=plc:crc`; Lookups: `approved_plc_crc`, `approved_plc_crc.csv`.
visualization: Table, Timeline, Single value (mismatches/day). Key fields: `controller`, `crc`, `expected_crc`, `first_bad`, `program_name`, `user`.
knownFalsePositives: Zone communications during authorized engineering, vendor maintenance, or planned commissioning. PAM sessions from authorized engineering access, vendor remote support, or scheduled maintenance.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Historian or engineering export.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=ot_eng` CRC events; `approved_plc_crc.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Export CRC after each validated download; (2) store gold images securely; (3) auto-ticket on mismatch; (4) offline forensic for first event.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot_eng sourcetype="plc:crc" earliest=-7d
| lookup approved_plc_crc.csv controller program_name OUTPUT expected_crc
| where isnotnull(expected_crc) AND crc!=expected_crc
| stats earliest(_time) as first_bad by controller, program_name, crc, expected_crc, user
| sort - first_bad
```

Understanding this SPL

**Unexpected PLC Logic CRC Change (IEC 62443-3-3 / SR 3.4)** — Compares reported logic checksums or project CRCs to last approved baseline to detect unauthorized software changes.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=ot_eng` CRC events; `approved_plc_crc.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Historian or engineering export. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot_eng; **sourcetype**: plc:crc. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot_eng, sourcetype="plc:crc", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(expected_crc) AND crc!=expected_crc` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by controller, program_name, crc, expected_crc, user** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the OT engineering record and session recording the control owner would use in an investigation. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Timeline, Single value (mismatches/day).
grandmaExplanation: We compare reported logic checksums or project CRCs to last approved baseline to detect unauthorized software changes. That helps protect factories, plants, and the gear that runs them from mistakes and bad actors the standards call out.
